content: Cursor Agent is by far the most polished IDE-based coding agent I've used. The inline diff preview alone is worth it.
Agreed. The multi-file editing is very smooth. My only concern is the proprietary nature — hard to customize deeply.
Have you tried it on a monorepo with 500+ files? I found it slows down noticeably there.
Aider is underrated. The git-aware editing means every change is tracked automatically, and I can always roll back.
The voice coding feature is surprisingly good for brainstorming sessions. Highly recommend trying it.
Devin's ability to handle full deployment pipelines is impressive. It went from zero to a working Vercel deploy in under 20 minutes.
The proprietary model is a deal-breaker for our team though. We need to audit the model weights for compliance.
Interesting. How does it handle existing complex codebases versus greenfield projects?
SWE-Agent's ACI design is brilliant. The custom shell commands reduce context window waste dramatically compared to raw shell output.
We integrated it into our CI pipeline for auto-fixing flaky tests. Works surprisingly well for that narrow use case.
LangChain Agent with LangGraph is a solid combination. The stateful workflow support is a game changer for complex pipelines.
The ecosystem is massive but can be overwhelming. Documentation has improved a lot though.
CrewAI makes multi-agent orchestration feel intuitive. The role-based design maps well to how real teams work.
I tried running a 5-agent crew for a research task. Coordination was smooth but token costs were significant.
AutoGen's group chat feature with dynamic speaker selection is really powerful for brainstorming-style agent workflows.
The human-in-the-loop integration is the best I've seen. You can seamlessly step in and out of the conversation.
OpenClaw's modular tool system has been great for our team. We built custom tools for internal APIs in under an hour.
The hot-reload for tools is a nice touch. How stable is the memory management on longer sessions?
AutoGPT was one of the first agents I tried. It's come a long way, but still gets stuck in loops on complex tasks.
The plugin ecosystem helps, but the core loop could use better plan validation before execution.
MetaGPT's structured output (PRD, design docs, code) is great for prototyping. Feels like having a small dev team on demand.
OpenDevin's sandboxed environment is excellent for security-conscious teams. We ran it on internal infra with confidence.
I'd love to see built-in support for more CI providers. Right now integrating with our Jenkins setup required custom work.
Cody's multi-repo understanding is a lifesaver for our microservice architecture. Context quality is noticeably better than competitors.
Tabby is the go-to option if you're serious about data privacy. No external API calls, everything runs on your hardware.
How does it compare to Continue with a locally hosted model? Both seem to target the self-hosted use case.
Continue's model-agnostic approach is great. We switched from GPT-4 to DeepSeek Coder and the slash commands still worked perfectly.
Sweep works well for small, well-defined issues. But it struggles when the issue description is vague or under-specified.
TaskWeaver is great for data analysis pipelines. The code-first approach feels natural for notebook-style exploration.
BabyAGI is more of a learning tool than a production system at this point, but the architecture is elegant and instructive.
